segments.s085-g57: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” The verb شود (becomes) is 3rd-person singular and comes before its subject مرد, a common poetic order; هنگام کار is an adverbial phrase “at the time of work” specifying the circumstance in which the man becomes lazy.
segments.s085-g58: ازان پس is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning “after that/thereafter.” نیابد is the present/relative-form of یافتن with a negative prefix ن- and here carries the force “(will) not find/obtain” in narrative usage. چنان modifies روزگار: “such a fortune/time/fate.”
segments.s085-g59: Two nouns (هیون تکاور) stand together in a compact, poetic NP: هیون describes a beastlike or savage creature and تکاور qualifies it as a raider/warrior. برآورد here is a 3rd-person singular past/preterite meaning “raised/spread” and takes پر (“wing/feather”) as its object: “(he) spread (his) wing(s).”
segments.s085-g60: بشد is a narrative past-form meaning “it happened / he came to be” (here: ‘he came’ or ‘approached’). نزد governs the following NP as ‘in the presence of’ or ‘before’ someone. سالار خورشید فر is a compounded title/epithet: سالار (chief/commander) followed by خورشید فر (literally ‘Sun’ + ‘glory’), giving “the chief, the Sun’s glory.”
segments.s085-g33: بیامد is a 3rd-person past singular verb ('he came'). چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). پیش functions as a locative/adverb meaning 'before' or 'in front of' and combines with دهستان ('the village / rural district') as the place reached. رسید is the 3rd-person past of 'to reach/arrive', so the clause reads 'when he reached before the village.' Note the verb-first initial بیامد is narrative—then a subordinate temporal clause follows.
segments.s085-g34: برابر here means 'opposite' / 'in front of' (it marks spatial opposition). سراپرده‌ای is سراپرده with the indefinite -ای ('an/one inner-chamber or inner-curtain/tent'); the noun is definite enough by context but marked as indefinite in form. برکشید is the 3rd-person past ('he raised/erected / drew up'), so this whole phrase means 'he raised an inner curtain/tent opposite (it).'
segments.s085-g35: سپه را uses را as a definite-object / topical marker: 'as for the host/army.' که is functioning rhetorically as 'who' (a rhetorical question). دانست is the past of 'to know' and, with the following کردن as a light verb, forms the idea 'who knew how to (do) the counting' — i.e., 'who could count them.' This is an exclamatory, rhetorical construction.
segments.s085-g36: برو is an imperative 'go' used as an exhortation. چارصد بار is a numeric adverbial 'four hundred times' (بار = 'time/occasion'). بشمر is a jussive/imperative form of 'to count' (present subjunctive/jussive marked with the prefix بِ-), so the sequence means 'Go, count four hundred times; count a thousand' (the final هزار in the line functions as the object/numeral being counted). The order places the frequency/adverbial before the verb, a typical poetic word order.
segments.s085-g37: بجوشید is a past/plural (or narrative) form meaning 'they boiled/seethed/ surged' and is used figuratively of a great movement. گفتی is parenthetical and here functions like 'you might say' or 'one would say' (a conventional poetic insertion introducing a comparison); همه = 'all'. ریگ و شخ enumerates what 'all' consists of ('sand and shakh' — شخ is an onomatopoeic/uncertain word denoting small particles or dust). The phrase paints a picture of everything surging as sand and dust.
segments.s085-g38: بیابان سراسر is 'the desert throughout' or 'the entire wilderness' (سراسر = 'throughout, entire'). چو is a simile marker 'like/as', and مور و ملخ are the terms being compared to ('ants and locusts'). The clause therefore says the whole desert was like ants and locusts — a vivid simile for a teeming crowd.
segments.s085-g39: This short enumerative phrase gives a person and a number: شاه نوذر = 'King Nowzar.' صد و چل هزار is the numeric expression 'one hundred and forty thousand' (صد = 100, چل = 40, هزار = thousand). The initial ابا is archaic/uncertain in meaning here (not fitting the provided senses neatly) and is best viewed as an exclamatory/colloquial filler; it does not affect the grammatical core 'شاه نوذر صد و چل هزار' which states the king and his number.
segments.s085-g40: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily' or 'indeed' introducing the statement. که links the emphatic particle to the following clause ('that/that indeed'), and بودند is the 3rd-person plural past of 'to be' agreeing with 'سوار' ('they were'). جنگی is an adjective meaning 'warlike' or 'military', modifying سوار ('riders/horsemen'), so the clause reads 'indeed there were warlike horsemen.'
segments.s085-g49: This clause uses the infinitive ستودان (“to praise”) as the verbal complement of سازدش. سازدش is سازد + ـش (3rd‑person object clitic), so the verb means “(he) makes him (do something). همی is the continual/progressive particle (“continually/keeps on”). The proper name زال appears after the verb cluster as the subject (poetic word order), and زر is the object of ستودان (‘praise gold’).
segments.s085-g50: ندارد is the negative of داشتن (“to have”) and here means “does not have.” همی again gives an ongoing sense (“still/ever”). جنگ را marks ‘war/battle’ with the object marker را (here topical/definite). پای و پر is a two‑term collocation (literally “foot and feather”) meaning ‘foothold/means or strength’ in battle; Persian places these nouns after ندارد as its negated objects.
segments.s085-g51: مرا (written here split as م + را) marks the experiencer/object (‘to me’ → ‘I’) and بیم is the noun ‘fear.’ ازو is a genitive/agentive ‘of/from him,’ and بد is a predicative adjective ‘bad’ meaning ‘harmful.’ به ایران‌زمین is the prepositional phrase ‘for/to the land of Iran.’ The overall construction expresses “I fear that he would be bad for Iran.”
segments.s085-g52: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when/if,’ او is ‘he,’ and شد is the perfective/become verb ‘become/happen.’ After the comma ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/through,’ here introducing the domain (ایران). بجوییم is 1st‑person‑plural present/subjunctive (“we seek/let us seek” or ‘we will seek’ in this classical register), and کین is the noun ‘vengeance.’ The clause says that if/when he comes to be so, we will seek vengeance (throughout) Iran.
segments.s085-g53: همانا is an emphatic particle placed at the clause head meaning 'verily' or 'indeed'. شماساس functions here as the subject (a proper name or title); در نیمروز is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at midday' following the subject (poetic word order).
segments.s085-g54: نشستست is an archaic 3rd-person singular perfect/past-form meaning 'he sat' or 'he is seated' (the -ست ending marks the old third person). با is the preposition 'with'. تاج گیتی فروز is a noun phrase where تاج 'crown' is modified by the following compound/phrase گیتی فروز 'world-illuminating' (فروز used attributively: 'that lights/brightens the world').
segments.s085-g55: به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of'; هر کار = 'every/any task or affair'. جستن is the infinitive 'to seek' placed before the predicate نکوست, an older impersonal/copular construction meaning 'is good' (so together: 'it is good to seek' or 'seeking is good').
segments.s085-g56: زدن رای is an idiomatic infinitival phrase: زدَن ('to strike/express') combines with رای ('counsel/opinion') to mean 'to give or take counsel, to consult'. با introduces the companion 'with'; مرد هشیار و دوست = 'a man who is alert/sensible and (a) friend/trusted one' (two coordinated modifiers on مرد).
segments.s085-g1: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause دشت از گیا گشت is literally 'the plain became of vegetation' (subject دشت + prepositional/material phrase از گیا + past verb گشت = 'became/turned'); this is a common poetic order where the verb follows its material phrase. The following چون + پرنیان is a simile 'like a silken (cloth)'.
segments.s085-g2: ببستند is past, 3rd-person-plural (they closed/formed). گردان توران is the noun phrase 'the Turanian squadrons/troops' (گردان = troops, توران = Turan) and میان functions adverbially as 'in the middle/in the midst'. Word order places the verb first (poetic inversion) followed by the troop phrase and the locative adverb.
segments.s085-g3: سپاهی is a collective noun 'an army' (used as the subject). بیامد is simple past 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and links the following names of peoples: ترکان و چین ('the Turks and China'). The conjunction و simply joins the two origins.
segments.s085-g4: هم is an additive particle 'also/too' introducing another source. از is 'from'. گرزداران (plural) means 'mace-bearers' or 'those armed with maces'; خاور زمین is a compound 'the eastern land/country' (خاور = east, زمین = land), so the whole phrase means 'from the mace‑bearers of the eastern land.'
segments.s085-g5: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause here ('for/that'). آن را is a topicalized 'it' with the classical object/topic marker را (used here to mark the theme). میان و کرانه نبود is a negated existential/predicative clause with نبود (past 'was not/existed not'): 'there was no middle and no margin/shore.'
segments.s085-g6: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that same' or 'the very'. بخت نوذر is a genitive phrase 'Nowzar's fortune/fate' (noun + possessor). جوانه نبود uses the metaphorical noun جوانه 'bud/incipient stage' plus نبود 'was not' to mean 'had not yet begun/sprouted' — i.e. that fate had not yet emerged.
segments.s085-g7: چو is the temporal 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. به نزدیک جیحون is a prepositional locative phrase 'to/near the Jihun' (به + نزدیک + place). رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived.' This is a typical classical word order: temporal marker + subject + locative + verb.
segments.s085-g8: خبر is 'news/report' and رسید is again 'reached/arrived'. نزد here means 'in the presence of' or 'to' (i.e. the news arrived at someone's presence). پور فریدون is the addressee/recipient ('Pur Fereydun' = son/descendant of Fereydun or a name), so the whole phrase means 'the news reached Pur Fereydun.'
segments.s085-g25: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking a partitive (’of the warriors’). جنگ‌آوران is a compound: جنگ (war) + آوران (bringers) = 'war-bringers', i.e. 'warriors'; مرد follows as a noun in apposition ('men/menfolk'). چون before a number functions as a comparative/approximative 'as/approximately' (classical usage meaning 'as many as' / 'about'). The whole segment lists who and how many proceeded.
segments.s085-g26: برفتند is the past/pluperfect narrative form 'they went/advanced' (third person plural). شایسته is an adjective meaning 'worthy/fit' and modifies the following noun. کارزار is the object noun 'battle/battlefield' so شایسته کارزار = 'fit for battle' (adjective + noun). Together the verb completes the clause: 'they went forth, fit for battle.'
segments.s085-g27: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. زابلستان is a toponym (Zabulistan) and is the goal of motion. نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/set' (here part of the light verb construction 'نهادنِ روی' = 'to turn/set one's face'). روی ('face') is the object of نهادند and together yields 'they set/turned their faces toward Zabulistan.' Note the object (روی) follows the verb, a common classical word order.
segments.s085-g28: ز کینه is an ablative/causal phrase 'out of hatred' (ز = 'from/because of', کینه = 'hatred'). به is the preposition 'to/toward' introducing دستان ('hands'); here دستان (hands) likely stands for action/arms and forms part of the idiom with نهادند روی ('they set their faces'), i.e. 'they turned (their faces) toward action/raised their hands.' نهادند is the same past verb 'they set', and روی again is 'face' (the object). Grammatically this is a repeated construction echoing the previous segment, with ز کینه specifying motive.
segments.s085-g29: خبر شد is the common narrative phrase 'news came' or 'it became known'. که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. The sequence سام نریمان is the name (subject of the embedded clause). بمرد is the classical/perhaps optative-looking form used in narrative complements meaning 'died' or 'that he died' — here it reports the event (he had died).
segments.s085-g30: همی is a classical narrative particle often giving immediacy ('then/at once' or 'indeed'). دخمه سازد is a verb phrase '(he) makes/builds a tomb' (سازد in narrative present used for past events). ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'for him/unto him'. زال is the agent/name placed after the object for emphasis, and گرد here functions in an older idiom meaning 'set about / went about' (i.e. Zal set about making the tomb). In other words, Zal undertook the building of the tomb for him.
segments.s085-g31: ازان is a particle 'from that / thereupon' linking this reaction to the previous fact. سخت here is an adverbial intensifier 'very/strongly' (classical usage). شادان شد is the verb phrase 'became joyful' (شادان = joyful, شد = became), and افراسیاب is the subject (name placed after the predicate, a common poetic order). The segment reports Afrāsīyāb's strong joy as a result.
segments.s085-g32: بدید is the past verb 'he saw'. آنکه is the relative 'the one who / he who' introducing a subordinate description. بخت is 'fortune/fate' and اندر آمد literally 'came into' — together بخت اندر آمد به خواب means 'fortune came to (someone) in a dream' (i.e. a dream foretelling good luck). Grammatically the clause describes the person Afrāsīyāb saw: 'the one to whom fate had come in a dream.'
segments.s085-g17: This is a noun phrase in which سراپرده (literally 'inner-chamber' or 'chief tent') is the head noun and نوذر شهریار follows as a name plus title: نوذر (Nowzar) + شهریار (king). Word order (head noun before the name/title) is common in epic/fixed phrases.
segments.s085-g18: کشیدند is a past-plural verb ('they drew/they set up/pitched') agreeing with an implicit plural subject (the army or attendants). The prepositional phrase بر دشت پیش حصار ('on the plain before the rampart/fortress') follows the verb: بر = 'on/upon', دشت = 'plain', پیش = 'before/in front of', حصار = 'fortress/rampart'. The verb + location word order (verb then place) is typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s085-g19: خود is an emphatic pronoun meaning 'he himself' (emphasis on the subject). اندر دهستان is an archaic/pre-classical locative phrase ('in/within Dehestan'); اندر is the older form of modern در. نیاراست here functions as a past-form adjective/verb meaning 'unready / not properly outfitted' (poetic/archaic formation related to آراستن), and جنگ is the governed noun 'war' (object/predicate). The clause is verbless or uses نیاراست predicatively to describe the state of the war or of the subject in that place.
segments.s085-g20: برین = بر این ('on/with this'); the orthographic element ین is the old/poetic form of این. بر نیامد is a compound past construction: نیامد ('did not come') with the preposition بر, here idiomatically 'no X came' — followed by زمانی درنگ ('a delay of time') meaning 'any delay'. Together the segment means 'upon this no delay came' i.e. 'there was no delay about that.' The word order (prepositional phrase + نیامد + noun) is idiomatic and common in older Persian.
segments.s085-g21: که here introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that' / 'who'); افراسیاب is the proper name (subject) and اندر ایران‌زمین is the locative adjunct 'in the land of Iran' using archaic اندر for 'in'. The sequence is a standard narrative relative: 'that Afrasiab, in Iran...'.
segments.s085-g22: دو سالار کرد is a simple past transitive clause: دو سالار ('two chiefs/commanders') is the object and کرد is the past of 'to do/make' meaning 'appointed / made (them)'. از بزرگان گزین is a source/qualification phrase: از بزرگان ('from the great men') and گزین (here functioning adjectivally/participially as 'chosen' / 'select') — together 'chosen from the great ones.' The verb کرد governs the whole; گزین is the participial modifier placed after the noun phrase.
segments.s085-g23: شماساس و دیگر خزروان is a coordinated noun phrase ('Shamasas and the other Khazruvans'). گرد here is an archaic/poetic particle that shades the idea of 'together/collected' or can mark the group; the whole segment names the recipients of an action (they are the people who receive the troops). The proper names/titles are presented before the verb as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s085-g24: ز لشکر سواران = 'horsemen from the army' where ز (poetic from) introduces the source (لشکر = army) modifying سواران (riders/horsemen). بدیشان is the dative phrase 'to them' (ب + ایشان = 'to/for them'), and سپرد is the past transitive 'entrusted/handed over.' The verb comes at the end (سپرد), typical of Persian word order (SOV with post-verbal particles in older style).
segments.s085-g41: به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'toward the army' (به = toward + لشکر = army). نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression: نگه ('a look, gaze') + کرد (past auxiliary 'did/made') = 'looked/kept watch'. The subject افراسیاب follows the verb, a common poetic word order (V — S here).
segments.s085-g42: هیونی (plur./collective 'wild beast(s)') is the direct object; برافگند is a past tense verb meaning 'he hurled/sent forth' (brafkandan). هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase 'at the time of sleep' = 'while (they) slept'. The subject remains Afrasiab from the previous clause (omitted here).
segments.s085-g43: یکی is an indefinite 'someone/a certain one'; نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بنوشت is the past verb 'wrote' (archaic/poetic past form of نوشتن); سوی + proper name پشنگ = 'to Pashang' (direction/recipient). Word order is typical poetic: object and verb close together, recipient after سوی.
segments.s085-g44: که introduces a reported-content clause 'that ...'. جستیم here takes the sense 'we sought' (classical/poetic use of جستن = to seek) and is first-person plural. نیکی = 'goodness/good fortune'. و آمد به چنگ is a short clause 'and it came into (our) grasp' (آمد = came; به + چنگ = 'into the hand/grasp').
segments.s085-g45: همه لشکر نوذر is a noun phrase 'the whole army of Nowzar'. ار is a conditional 'if' (classical/poetic); بشکریم is a first-person-plural verb form following that conditional meaning 'we [do X]' — here context requires 'defeat/rout' (the exact lexical annotation provided does not show that sense, so the specific lexical sense is not available in the supplied list).
segments.s085-g46: شکارند is a present/plural predicate 'they are prey / they will be taken as prey'. و is the conjunction 'and'. در زیر پی is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'under the foot/underfoot' (در = in/under; زیر = beneath; پی here = 'foot'). بسپریم is a first-person-plural verb 'we consign/hand over' (here used idiomatically of throwing/trampling underfoot).
segments.s085-g47: دگر here = 'then/next' or 'another' functioning adverbially; سام is the proper name (subject). رفت is the simple past 'went/left'. از در شهریار is a genitive/compound locative 'from the court/door of the king' (از = from; در = gate/door/presence; شهریار = the king). Word order places the verb before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s085-g48: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/surely'. نیاید is an archaic/poetic negative-present/subjunctive form (نـ + آید) meaning 'will not come / should not come'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'to this' (here shown as separate elements; ب = 'to' and دین is likely a transcriptional issue for این = 'this'), and کارزار = 'battle/fight'. So the clause means 'surely he will not come to this battle.'
segments.s085-g9: سپاه جهاندار is a noun phrase where سپاه (noun) is qualified by the epithet جهاندار placed after it ('the army of the sovereign'). بیرون شدند is a past plural verbal phrase: بیرون (an adverb/locative 'out') + شدند (past plural of شدن), here functioning as a lexical verb meaning 'went out/issued forth'.
segments.s085-g10: ز introduces source ('from') before the noun phrase کاخ همایون ('the august/imperial palace'); به + هامون marks direction ('to/toward the plain'). The verb شدند repeats at line end and agrees with the plural subject (سپاه), so the clause means they went from the palace to the plain.
segments.s085-g11: به راه دهستان نهادند روی is an idiomatic locative-directional phrase: به راه دهستان ('onto the road toward Dehestan') + نهادند (past plural of نهادن 'to set/put') + روی (literally 'face'). Together نهادند روی means 'they set their face / turned their course' toward that road — a poetic way to say 'they set out for Dehestan.'
segments.s085-g12: سپهدارشان is سپهدار ('commander') with the possessive suffix -شان 'their' ('their commander'). The following قارن is a proper name in apposition (Qaran), and رزم‌جوی is an epithet 'warrior/battle‑seeking' modifying him; Persian often places such epithets after the name.
segments.s085-g13: شهنشاه نوذر names the king (شهنشاه 'emperor/king' + the proper name نوذر). پس پشت اوی is an archaic/poetic locative phrase: پس 'behind/after' + پشت 'back/rear' + اوی (archaic pronoun 'him/his'), meaning 'behind him' or 'at his rear'.
segments.s085-g14: This clause has an omitted copula (poetic zero 'to be'). جهانی سراسر 'the whole world' (جهانی modified by سراسر 'throughout') is the subject phrase, followed by پر از گفت و گوی, a 'full of' construction: پر از ('full of') + گفت و گوی ('talk/conversation'). Together: 'the whole world was full of talk.'
segments.s085-g15: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر (subject) + به پیش دهستان ('to/in front of Dehestan') is the locative phrase, and رسید is the past of رسیدن 'arrived/reached'. The clause means 'when the army reached the approach of Dehestan.'
segments.s085-g16: تو گفتی که introduces reported speech ('you said that'); the subordinate clause خورشید شد ناپدید uses شد + adjective (ناپدید) to mean 'became/disappeared' — literally 'the sun became invisible/vanished.' Note the word order شد ناپدید (verb then adjective) which is acceptable in older/poetic Persian.

sentences.s085-s29: When a man grows lazy at the time of work, thereafter he will not find such a fortune.
sentences.s085-s30: The savage raider spread his wings and came into the presence of the chief, the Sun's glory.
sentences.s085-s17: He came; when he reached before the village, he raised an inner curtain/tent opposite (it).
sentences.s085-s18: Who could count the host! Go — count four hundred times; count a thousand.
sentences.s085-s19: They surged — you might say — all sand and shakh; the whole desert was like ants and locusts.
sentences.s085-s20: Now, King Nowzar had one hundred and forty thousand; verily, there were warlike horsemen.
sentences.s085-s25: Zāl continually makes him praise gold; he has no foothold or strength for battle.
sentences.s085-s26: I fear him; it would be bad for the land of Iran — if that happens, we shall seek vengeance across Iran!
sentences.s085-s27: Verily Shamāsās sat at midday wearing the world-illuminating crown.
sentences.s085-s28: At the time of any undertaking, it is good to seek counsel: consult with a wise and trusted man.
sentences.s085-s1: When the plain turned green with vegetation and lay like a silken cloth, the Turanian squadrons closed up in the middle.
sentences.s085-s2: An army came from the Turks and from China, and also from the mace‑bearers of the eastern land.
sentences.s085-s3: For it had neither centre nor border; that same fortune of Nowzar had not yet budded.
sentences.s085-s4: When the host reached near the Jihun, the news reached (came) to Pur Fereydun.
sentences.s085-s13: Of the warriors about thirty thousand men went forth, fit for battle.
sentences.s085-s14: They turned their faces toward Zabulistan; out of hatred they set their faces to (raise) their hands.
sentences.s085-s15: News came that Sam Nārīmān had died; Zal at once set about making a tomb for him.
sentences.s085-s16: From that Afrāsīyāb became greatly joyful; he saw the one to whom fate had come in a dream.
sentences.s085-s9: They pitched King Nowzar's main tent on the plain before the fortress.
sentences.s085-s10: He himself in Dehestan had not prepared for the war; nevertheless there came no delay in that matter.
sentences.s085-s11: Afrasiab, in the land of Iran, appointed two commanders, chosen from the great men.
sentences.s085-s12: He entrusted to Shamasas and the other Khazruvans a detachment of horsemen from the army.
sentences.s085-s21: Afrasiab looked toward the host and sent forth wild beasts while (they) slept.
sentences.s085-s22: Someone wrote a letter to Pashang: that we sought good fortune and it came into our grasp.
sentences.s085-s23: If we rout Nowzar's entire army, they will be taken as prey, and we will consign them underfoot.
sentences.s085-s24: Then Sam left the king's presence; surely he will not come to this battle.
sentences.s085-s5: The sovereign's army came out of the august palace and moved onto the plain.
sentences.s085-s6: They set out on the road to Dehestan; their commander was Qaran, the battle‑seeking warrior.
sentences.s085-s7: King Nowzar — behind him the whole world was filled with talk.
sentences.s085-s8: When the army reached before Dehestan, you said that the sun had become hidden.
